narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 92% chance that you would not pass based on your academic profile. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your third-year grade decile, which ranked in the bottom tier and significantly pushed the prediction toward failure, unlike the mid-to-upper deciles typical among students who passed the bar. Your LSAT score of 26 was the second-biggest factor, as it fell well below the average of about 37 for successful bar takers, further increasing the model's concern about your exam readiness. Rounding out the top influences, your first-year grade decile also landed at the lowest level, compared to an average around the sixth decile for those who passed, adding to the overall risk signal. While your upper-middle family income aligns with many who succeed and your choice of full-time study matches the norm for passers, these did not outweigh the academic red flags. In contrast, admitted students typically show much stronger early and later undergrad performance, positioning them as low-risk for the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where low deciles like yours correlated strongly with bar struggles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 37+ range seen in successful applicants, and focus on improving your grades to climb into higher deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional coursework. You might also explore part-time programs or gap-year experiences that build your academic record. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation emphasizing your resilience could help reframe your story in future applications. With deliberate steps to address these key areas, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next opportunity.
